terms.definition: Literally 'connection' or 'joining'. The annexation of Austria into Nazi Germany on 12 March 1938. The term refers to the political union that had been forbidden by the Treaty of Versailles. A plebiscite conducted under occupation produced a reported 99.73% approval.

Literally 'lightning war'. The combined-arms military strategy using armoured vehicles, motorised infantry, and air support to overwhelm and penetrate enemy defences before they could reorganise. Applied effectively in Poland (1939) and France (1940). The term was used more by Allied observers and journalists than by German commanders themselves.

The German Labour Front, established 10 May 1933 after the Nazis dissolved all trade unions. Led by Robert Ley, it enrolled over 25 million workers by 1939 — the largest mass organisation in the Third Reich. The DAF ran the Kraft durch Freude (Strength through Joy) leisure programme, which provided subsidised holidays, cultural events, and the Volkswagen savings scheme.
terms.origin: German: Deutsche Arbeitsfront — German Labour Front

terms.definition: SS mobile killing units that followed the Wehrmacht into occupied Soviet territories from 1941. They were responsible for the massacre of approximately 1.5–2 million Jews, Roma, Soviet officials, and others — primarily by mass shooting into pits. The largest single operation, Babi Yar (29–30 September 1941, near Kyiv), killed 33,771 Jews in two days.

The law passed by the Reichstag on 23 March 1933 that gave Hitler's cabinet the power to enact laws without Reichstag approval for four years. Passed with Centre Party support after Communist deputies were arrested or absent. It effectively ended the Weimar Republic and transferred legislative power to Hitler. Renewed in 1937 and 1939; superseded by Hitler's wartime powers.

The Nazi regime's plan for the systematic genocide of the Jews of Europe. The phrase 'final solution of the Jewish question' (Endlösung der Judenfrage) was used at the Wannsee Conference of January 1942, which coordinated the industrial-scale murder. The Holocaust resulted in the death of approximately six million Jews — two-thirds of European Jewry — in shooting operations, gas vans, and purpose-built extermination camps.
terms.origin: German: Endlösung — final solution, final resolution

terms.definition: German for 'leader'. Hitler's official title from August 1934, combining the offices of Chancellor and President after Hindenburg's death. It carried connotations of an absolute, messianic, quasi-divine leadership, distinct from all normal political authority. Germans were required to use 'Heil Hitler' as a greeting from 1933.

The regional NSDAP leaders, each responsible for one of Germany's 43 Gaue (districts). Gauleiters combined party authority with growing state power (often serving as Reich Governors), controlled regional economic and military resources, and wielded enormous local power. Some, like Josef Grünpfner of East Prussia (Erich Koch) or Fritz Sauckel of Thuringia, became de facto regional dictators, particularly in occupied eastern territories.

The Secret State Police of Nazi Germany. Founded in Prussia by Göring in April 1933, transferred to SS control under Heydrich in 1934. Operated without judicial oversight, using surveillance, denunciations, interrogation, and extra-judicial detention to suppress political opposition. A criminal organisation as determined at Nuremberg. Contrary to popular image, the Gestapo was relatively small (roughly 32,000 officers for all of Germany in 1944) and depended extensively on civilian denunciations.
terms.origin: German acronym: Geheime Staatspolizei — Secret State Police

terms.definition: Literally 'coordination' or 'bringing into line'. The process by which the Nazis brought all German institutions — political parties, trade unions, press, judiciary, universities, professional bodies, cultural organisations — under party control between 1933 and 1934. The term comes from electrical engineering, describing the alignment of circuits.
terms.origin: German: gleich (same, equal) + schalten (to switch, connect)

terms.definition: The systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million Jews by the Nazi regime and its collaborators between 1933 and 1945. The term 'Holocaust' (from Greek: whole burned offering) became predominant in English from the 1960s. The Hebrew term 'Shoah' (catastrophe) is preferred by many Jewish communities. The murder also encompassed approximately 250,000–500,000 Roma, 275,000+ disabled persons, 1.8–3 million Soviet POWs, and hundreds of thousands of political prisoners and others.
terms.origin: Greek: holókaustos — wholly burned; Hebrew equivalent: Shoah (catastrophe)

terms.definition: Literally 'Night of Crystal' or 'Night of Broken Glass'. The coordinated nationwide pogrom against Jews on 9–10 November 1938, ordered by Goebbels with Hitler's approval following the assassination of German diplomat Ernst vom Rath in Paris by Herschel Grynszpan. Over 7,500 Jewish businesses destroyed, ~1,400 synagogues burned or damaged, 91 Jews killed (official figure; actual deaths higher), 30,000 arrested and sent to camps. Jews were subsequently ordered to pay a 1-billion-Reichsmark 'atonement fine'.

Literally 'Strength through Joy'. The DAF's leisure organisation, designed to provide subsidised recreation for German workers — cruises, theatre, concerts, sports, and the famous KdF-Wagen (Volkswagen) savings scheme. It was also an instrument of mass surveillance, monitoring workers' political reliability. By 1938, KdF organised more leisure travel than any organisation in the world.

Literally 'living space'. The geopolitical concept that Germany required vast territories to the east (primarily the Soviet Union and Poland) for German agricultural settlement, resource exploitation, and demographic expansion. Developed by geographer Friedrich Ratzel in the 1890s; adopted and radicalised by Hitler, who combined it with antisemitism (Bolshevism as Jewish conspiracy) and Social Darwinism. The concept provided the ideological framework for Operation Barbarossa and the murderous occupation policies in the east.

Hitler's autobiographical political manifesto, dictated to Rudolf Hess during his imprisonment at Landsberg following the failed Beer Hall Putsch. Published in two volumes: vol. 1 (1925) and vol. 2 (1926). It outlined his life story, his ideology (antisemitism, German nationalism, anti-Marxism, Lebensraum), and his strategic vision. By 1945, 12.4 million copies had been distributed — given free to every German newlywed couple and soldier. Despite clear statements of intent, Allied leaders did not take its contents seriously before 1939.

terms.term: Night of the Long Knives (Nacht der langen Messer)
terms.definition: The purge of 30 June–2 July 1934 in which Hitler ordered the murder of SA leadership and other perceived threats. At least 85 killed, including SA Chief of Staff Ernst Röhm, former Chancellor Kurt von Schleicher, and Gregor Strasser. The killings were retroactively legalised as 'state self-defence'. The purge eliminated the SA as a political force, confirmed SS supremacy, and secured the Wehrmacht's cooperation with the regime — in exchange for the oath of personal loyalty sworn after Hindenburg's death.
terms.origin: German literary allusion to an English folk saying; popularised by Goebbels's propaganda.

terms.definition: Two antisemitic laws enacted at the Nuremberg party rally on 15 September 1935: (1) the Reich Citizenship Law (Reichsbürgergesetz), stripping Jews of German citizenship; (2) the Law for the Protection of German Blood and Honour (Gesetz zum Schutze des deutschen Blutes und der deutschen Ehre), forbidding marriage and sexual relations between Jews and non-Jews. Supplementary decrees defined who counted as a Jew (one Jewish grandparent sufficient in some cases). The laws provided the legal basis for progressive economic and social exclusion.
terms.origin: Named for Nuremberg, where the annual NSDAP congress was held.

terms.definition: Literally 'Order Castle'. Three elite training schools for NSDAP political leaders: Vogelsang (Eifel), Sonthofen (Allgäu), and Krössinsee (Pomerania). Named after medieval Teutonic Order castles, they provided political and physical training for the future Nazi leadership elite, selected from the Hitler Youth. The Ordensburgen were among the most lavishly funded party projects; graduates were intended to form the leadership cadre of the coming Thousand-Year Reich.
terms.origin: German: Orden (order, as in military/religious order) + Burg (castle, fortress)

terms.definition: The German parliament building and legislative body. After the February 1933 fire and the Enabling Act, the Reichstag retained only ceremonial functions, meeting occasionally to hear Hitler's speeches or to rubber-stamp major announcements. The building itself was not repaired; sessions were held in the Kroll Opera House across the square. After 1942 the Reichstag did not meet at all.
terms.origin: German: Reich (empire, realm) + Tag (day, as in assembly day)

terms.definition: Literally 'Storm Detachment'. The NSDAP's original paramilitary wing, known as 'Brownshirts' for their uniform. Founded 1921, the SA used violence to disrupt political meetings, intimidate opponents, and attack Jews. At its peak (1934) it had 4.5 million members — by far the largest military force in Germany. Its political ambitions under Ernst Röhm (absorbing the Wehrmacht) led to the Night of the Long Knives (1934), after which it became a ceremonial organisation of no political significance.

Literally 'Protection Squadron'. Founded 1925 as Hitler's personal bodyguard (9 members); grown to a million-man empire under Heinrich Himmler by 1944. The SS was the ideological and operational vanguard of the Nazi racial state: it ran the concentration and extermination camps, controlled the Gestapo, deployed the Einsatzgruppen, fielded the Waffen-SS military force, and administered occupied territories. Declared a criminal organisation at Nuremberg.

Literally 'people's community'. The Nazi concept of a racially homogeneous German national community bound by blood and soil, from which Jews, Roma, disabled persons, homosexuals, and political opponents were systematically excluded. It was also a mobilising ideal promising to overcome class divisions through racial solidarity — an appeal that resonated with many during the Depression. In practice, its enforcement required surveillance, informants, and terror.

The armed wing of the SS that served alongside the Wehrmacht during the war. Formed from SS-VT (Special Purpose Troops, 1933) and SS-Totenkopfverbände (Death's Head Units, who guarded camps). By 1945 comprised 38 divisions totalling nearly 900,000 men from Germany and occupied/allied nations across Europe. Elite Waffen-SS units (Leibstandarte, Das Reich, Totenkopf) fought with distinction by military standards while committing documented war crimes including the Malmedy massacre and the massacre at Oradour-sur-Glane.

The unified armed forces of Nazi Germany (1935–1945), comprising the Heer (army), Kriegsmarine (navy), and Luftwaffe (air force), with the SS as a parallel military force. The term replaced the earlier Reichswehr. At its peak the Wehrmacht fielded over 9 million men. The Wehrmacht's complicity in war crimes on the Eastern Front — particularly regarding Soviet POWs (3.3 million died in captivity) and civilian reprisals — has been extensively documented by historians.
